forecasts.nextMove: Global instability may force further hikes

rba.note: Two consecutive hikes in 2026 (Feb + Mar). Cash rate at 4.10%, highest since 2024. Markets pricing 85% chance of another hike in May. Hawkish RBA language on inflation.

unemployment.trend: rising slightly — trend at 4.2%, seasonally adjusted 4.3%

employment.note: Unemployment ticking up from 4.1% to 4.3% seasonally adjusted. Labour market softening but not collapsing. March data due 16 April.

inflation.note: Still well above RBA 2-3% target. Housing +7.2%, electricity +37% (rebate distortion). Trimmed mean sticky at 3.3%. This is why RBA is hiking.

wages.note: Wages growth 3.4% vs inflation 3.7% = real wages still negative. Private sector 3.4%, public 4.0%. Next release May 2026.

migration.forecast2026: NOM expected to fall to ~260,000 in 2026 (gov't Population Statement)
migration.note: 306k NOM still well above long-run average (~190k). WA and QLD growing fastest. Population 27.6M. Even at forecast 260k, demand pressure remains strong vs undersupply.

approvals.note: Feb 2026 spike to 19,022 was apartment-driven. Houses steady at ~9,847. Trailing 12mo total 195k vs 240k target = persistent undersupply. Good for existing property values.

vacancy.note: National vacancy 1.1% is a multi-year low. Perth 0.6%, Adelaide 0.5%, Brisbane 0.6% — all extremely tight. Only Melbourne (1.4%) showing any balance. Strong rental demand = yield support.

medians.note: Capital city median now $1.155M. Perth ($1.03M) and Adelaide ($981k) have surged past budget — BUT these are CITY-WIDE medians. Many suburbs within these cities remain under $800k. Darwin ($710k) and Hobart ($710k) fit budget at city-wide level.

cities.trend: limited auction market — private treaty dominant

clearanceRates.note: Clearance rates 65-72% across capitals — balanced market. Not overheated, not collapsing. Adelaide strongest. Easter weekend (4 April) skipped. Next data 11 April.

lending.apraChanges: APRA retained 3% buffer. New DTI cap: max 20% of new loans at 6x+ income, effective Feb 2026.
lending.note: Investor rates 5.59-6.60%. APRA assessment rate ~8.6-9.6% squeezes borrowing capacity. New DTI cap limits high-leverage lending. Rates rising with cash rate hikes.

risks.impact: Each 25bp hike reduces borrowing capacity ~3%. May hike 85% priced. Could reach 4.35% by mid-2026.
CPI 3.7% still well above 2-3% target. Housing and energy driving. Delays any rate relief.
Iran conflict + trade tensions could spike energy prices, push inflation higher, force more hikes.
Would cool demand significantly. Currently 4.3% and trending up slowly. Watch closely.
POSITIVE RISK: 195k approvals vs 240k target. Structural shortage supports prices even through headwinds.

summary: Challenging macro environment for investors: RBA hiking cycle resumed with back-to-back rises to 4.10%, inflation sticky at 3.7%, and more hikes likely. However, structural tailwinds remain strong — vacancy rates at multi-year lows (1.1% nationally), building approvals ~18% below target, and migration still elevated at 306k. Perth, Adelaide, and Brisbane vacancy rates (0.5-0.6%) signal extreme rental tightness. The key tension is between rising rates (reducing borrowing capacity) and persistent undersupply (supporting prices and rents). For a growth investor with $800k budget, the sweet spot is suburbs in WA/SA where rates haven't yet priced out opportunity.
warnings: RBA has hiked TWICE in 2026 already — reassess serviceability at 4.10% before committing
Perth and Adelaide city-wide medians now above $800k — must target specific suburbs
APRA's new DTI cap (6x income) may limit borrowing — check with broker
March CPI data (late April) could seal another May hike
